meta.handoff_type: Explorer → Lovász (v2: REVISED after steer:adjust + Lovász BL1/BL3 review)
meta.revision_note: v2 revision: KL ceiling corrected to 1/3 (not 1/2); top_gap changed to GAP2 (doubly-iterated Raghavan, most tractable); KL2 (tripartite grid norm) added as new gap; EHPS lower bound description corrected; van Corput corollary (r_4(N) from Raghavan) added as new observation. Explorer Finding F3 exponent '1/12' retracted — correct exponent is 1/6 with halved constant.

correction_1.what_was_wrong: Gap 1 frozen target was r_3(N) ≤ N·exp(-c(log N)^{1/2}), claiming Kelley-Lyu directly gives exponent 1/2 for 3-APs
correction_1.correction: Kelley-Lyu's √d trick gives exponent 1/3 (NOT 1/2) when adapted to 3-APs. In KL: d ~ log(N) → √d ~ (log N)^{1/2} → exponent 1/2. In Croot-Sisask for 3-APs: d = α^{-2} → √d = α^{-1} → L¹ rank O(α^{-1}) → exponent 1/(1·3) = 1/3. Exponent 1/2 via sifting requires 'Tripartite Grid Norm' (Conjecture KL2, 3-7 years, open).
correction_1.source: Kelley-Lyu analysis (research/kelley_lyu_analysis.md, §3-§5); confirmed by Lovász BL1 analysis
correction_1.probability_direct_transfer: 15-20% for direct port to give 1/2; ~50% for 1/3 via KL √d trick

correction_2.what_was_wrong: EHPS lower bound described as r_3(N) ≥ N·exp(-C(log N)^{1-ε})
correction_2.correction: EHPS (arXiv:2406.12290) improves the CONSTANT in Behrend's exp(-c·√(log N)), not the exponent structure. Correct: r_3(N) ≥ N·2^{-(2√(log₂(24/7))+o(1))·√(log₂N)} = N·exp(-c'·√(log N)) with c' ≈ 2.667. The exponent is 1/2 (same as Behrend), only the constant improved.

correction_3.correction: arXiv:2505.01587 was first posted May 2025 (v1). Latest revision June 2026. Cite as 'May 2025; updated June 2026'.

correction_4.what_was_wrong: Explorer Finding F3: van Corput gives r_4(N) ≤ N·exp(-(log N)^{1/12})
correction_4.correction: FORMULA ERROR. Correct: r_4(N) ≤ N·exp(-c/2·(log N)^{1/6}). The exponent of log N stays 1/6; only the constant halves. The '1/12' would require √(exp(-x^{1/6})) = exp(-x^{1/12}) which is false. Correct: √(exp(-c·x^{1/6})) = exp(-c/2·x^{1/6}).

new_result.label: Van Corput + Raghavan: first quasi-polynomial bound for r_4(N)
new_result.derivation: Van Corput: r_4(N) ≤ C√(N·r_3(N)) [folklore, valid via Gowers/Cauchy-Schwarz]; Raghavan 2026: r_3(N) ≤ C₁N·exp(-c(log N)^{1/6}); combined: r_4(N) ≤ CC₁^{1/2}·N·exp(-c/2·(log N)^{1/6})
new_result.significance: First quasi-polynomial bound for k=4, beating Green-Tao 2017 (N(logN)^{-c}) and LSS 2024 (N·exp(-(loglogN)^{c_4})). Exponent 1/6 is same as r_3(N), constant halved.
new_result.novelty_status: Appears to be a new observation not noted in Raghavan (2026) or subsequent literature
new_result.caution: The van Corput step requires the Gowers/Cauchy-Schwarz energy argument (not the direct 6-point argument which has a gap). The direct claim 'A_d is 3-AP-free when A is 4-AP-free' is false in general; what IS true is that some A_d is 3-AP-free and has size ≥ |A|²/(cN).

top_gap_for_lovász.label: Doubly-Iterated Raghavan: L¹ Density Increment for 3-APs
top_gap_for_lovász.ranking_reason: MOST TRACTABLE (1-3 years). The Sifting Hierarchy Formula confirms m=4 predicts exponent 1/3. BL1 analysis shows: ℓ¹ spectrum of 1_A has rank O(α^{-1}), but the standard density increment requires L² control. The KEY missing piece is an L¹ density increment for 3-APs on a rank O(α^{-1}) Bohr set. This is precisely stated and mechanically within reach of the current sifting framework.

second_gap_for_lovász.label: Tripartite Grid Norm Conjecture (path to exponent 1/2)
second_gap_for_lovász.ranking_reason: MEDIUM-TERM (3-7 years). If the KL √d trick can be extended to trilinear (3-variable) tensors via a 'tripartite grid norm', it would give exponent 1/2 for r_3(N), matching Behrend. No trilinear analogue of KL's bilinear decoupling is known. This is the correct long-term research target after GAP2.

new_conjectures.KL1: Bilinear Barrier: The KL √d trick is bilinear-specific; gives exponent 1/3 for any k-linear sifting with k ≥ 3.
new_conjectures.KL2: Tripartite Grid Norm: There exists a U(2,2,k) grid norm for tripartite arrays such that the KL sifting lemma extends, giving r_3(N) ≤ N·exp(-c(log N)^{1/2}). Status: NEW OPEN CONJECTURE (3-7 year target).
new_conjectures.KL3: Ceiling Dichotomy: The ceiling achievable by grid norm sifting is either exactly 1/3 (if tripartite reduction impossible) or exactly 1/2 (if KL2 provable). No intermediate value.

frozen_target.statement: r_3(N) ≤ N · exp(−c(log N)^{1/2}) for some absolute c > 0 [REVISED: ceiling via direct KL adaptation is 1/3, not 1/2; 1/2 requires KL2 conjecture]
frozen_target.level: Level 2 of the asymptotic program (matching Behrend lower bound exponent)
frozen_target.significance: Ultimate target; requires Tripartite Grid Norm Conjecture (KL2). Near-term target is exponent 1/3 via L¹ density increment (GAP2).
frozen_target.revised_ceiling: Direct KL √d adaptation to 3-APs gives exponent 1/3 (not 1/2). Bilinear structure (KL: 2-variable) vs trilinear (3-APs: 3-variable) is the key obstacle. No trilinear decoupling inequality known.
frozen_target.transfer_probability: 15-20% direct to 1/2; 50-60% for 1/3 via KL √d + L¹ density increment

r3_bound_exp_1_6.reference: Raghavan 2026 (arXiv:2603.27045), with extra (log log N)^{-1} factor

best_known_results.upper_bound: r_3(N) ≤ N · exp(−c · (log N)^{1/6} / log log N) [Raghavan 2026]
best_known_results.lower_bound: r_3(N) ≥ N · exp(−C · (log N)^{1/2}) [Behrend 1946, constant C ≈ 2.667 by EHPS 2024]
best_known_results.communication_complexity_analogue: In the bipartite communication complexity setting with 'grid norms', Kelley–Lyu achieve exponent 1/2 using the same sifting machinery

known_obstructions_failed_methods.why_failed: Gives only N / log log N; no quasi-polynomial bound
known_obstructions_failed_methods.ceiling: Exponent 0 in the N · exp(-(log N)^β) scale

known_obstructions_failed_methods.why_insufficient: Effective rank O(α^{-4}) gives exponent 1/12, not 1/2
Achieves the Croot–Sisask L² floor: effective rank O(α^{-2}), exponent 1/6. Cannot go further within L² framework.
known_obstructions_failed_methods.ceiling: 1/6 (with L² methods); the L² barrier prevents surpassing this without changing the norm

known_obstructions_failed_methods.fundamental_limitation: Rank d = O(α^{-2}) is TIGHT for L²: by Parseval, the number of Fourier frequencies ξ with |f̂(ξ)| ≥ εα² is at most O(1/(ε²α²)). Any improvement requires changing to L¹ or a different norm.

theorem_precondition_gaps.needed_for: Reducing effective rank to ρ=1, giving exponent 1/3; or ρ=0 for exponent 1/2
Porting Kelley–Lyu's grid-norm argument to 3-APs; requires showing 3-AP tensor has 'grid-like' structure

theorem_precondition_gaps.precondition: Understanding of what 'grid norm sifting' does mechanistically in Kelley–Lyu
theorem_precondition_gaps.needed_for: Any transfer argument; requires reading arXiv:2505.01587 in detail

actual_barrier_lemmas.name: Grid-Norm Croot–Sisask Lemma for 3-APs
actual_barrier_lemmas.precise_statement: Let A ⊆ Z_N with density α = |A|/N. Consider the 3-AP tensor T(a,b,c) = 1_A(a) · 1_A(b) · 1_A(c) · 1[a+c=2b]. For fixed b, define the bipartite function f_b(a,c) = 1_A(a) · 1_A(c) · 1[a+c=2b]. The 'grid norm' of f_b (analogous to Kelley–Lyu's grid norm) is ‖f_b‖_{grid} = (∑_a ∑_c f_b(a,c))^{1/2} / N. Claim: if ‖f_b‖_{grid} ≥ δ for many b, then there exists a Bohr set B = Bohr(Γ, ρ) of rank d = O(δ^{-1} · log(1/ε)) such that A is (ε, B)-almost-periodic — i.e., ‖1_A * μ_B - 1_A‖₁ ≤ ε · α · N. If true with d = O(α^{-1}), this gives exponent 1/3. If d = O(1) (independent of α), this gives exponent 1/2.
actual_barrier_lemmas.why_barrier: Whether the grid-norm sifting gives rank O(α^{-1}) or O(α^{-2}) for 3-APs depends on the TRIPARTITE vs. BIPARTITE structure. Kelley–Lyu's result in the bipartite setting gives O(α^{-1}). The question is whether tripartite 3-APs inherit this rank improvement.
actual_barrier_lemmas.key_question_for_lovász: Is the 3-AP incidence tensor T(a,b,c) = 1[a+c=2b] equivalent to a grid/bipartite structure after a suitable change of variables or decomposition?

actual_barrier_lemmas.name: L¹-Almost-Periodicity Lemma
actual_barrier_lemmas.precise_statement: Let f: Z_N → [-1,1] with E[f] = α. Then there exists a Bohr set B = Bohr(Γ, ρ) of rank d = O(α^{-1} · log(1/δ)) and ρ = Ω(α^{C·α^{-1}}) (exponentially small in rank, acceptable for the iteration) such that ‖f * μ_B - f‖₁ ≤ δ · ‖f‖₁. Equivalently: the set Spec_δ(f) = {ξ ∈ ℤ_N : |f̂(ξ)| ≥ δ · ‖f̂‖_∞} has |Spec_δ(f)| ≤ C · α^{-1} / δ.
actual_barrier_lemmas.why_barrier: The standard Croot–Sisask lemma proves this with rank O(α^{-2}) using L² Parseval. The L¹ analogue would give rank O(α^{-1}) using L¹ Parseval (Hausdorff–Young). The L¹ version requires non-negativity of f or a sign condition that may not hold in general.
actual_barrier_lemmas.key_question_for_lovász: Under what conditions on f does L¹-almost-periodicity hold with rank O(α^{-1})? Is non-negativity sufficient? Does the indicator function 1_A automatically satisfy these conditions?

actual_lemma_queue_seed.statement: The 3-AP counting condition Σ_{a,b,c} 1_A(a) 1_A(b) 1_A(c) 1[a+c=2b] can be reduced to a sum of bipartite grid norms: Σ_b |{(a,c): a+c=2b, a ∈ A, c ∈ A}| = Σ_b (1_A * 1_A)(2b). The function (1_A * 1_A)(2b) is a convolution evaluated at 2b, and its Fourier transform is |Â(ξ)|², which is non-negative. This non-negativity is a key structural property.
actual_lemma_queue_seed.significance: The non-negativity of |Â(ξ)|² might be the 'grid-like structure' that enables Kelley–Lyu's improvement. If so, the 3-AP problem IS a bipartite problem (from the Fourier perspective).

actual_lemma_queue_seed.statement: The 3-AP count Λ_3(A) = Σ_{a+c=2b} 1_A(a) 1_A(b) 1_A(c) equals (1/N) Σ_ξ Â(ξ)² · Â̄(2ξ). The term |Â(ξ)|² is non-negative. This positivity structure might be exploitable in the grid-norm framework.
actual_lemma_queue_seed.significance: This formula shows the 3-AP tensor DOES have a bipartite structure when expressed via Fourier: the 'Alice half' contributes Â(ξ)² (positive) and the 'Bob half' contributes Â̄(2ξ). Whether this constitutes a 'grid norm' in Kelley–Lyu's sense is the key question.

actual_lemma_queue_seed.statement: If f: Z_N → [0,1] (non-negative!), then the almost-periodicity Bohr set can be taken of rank d = O(‖f‖₁^{-1} · log(1/δ)) (using only |{ξ: |f̂(ξ)| ≥ δ · ‖f̂‖_∞}| ≤ ‖f‖₁/δ·‖f‖_∞ ≤ N/δ from L∞ bounds). But 1_A: Z_N → [0,1] is already non-negative! So the L¹ estimate might apply directly.
actual_lemma_queue_seed.significance: If 1_A being non-negative allows the L¹ spectral bound to apply, then the rank of the almost-periodicity Bohr set is O(α^{-1}), not O(α^{-2}). This would be the breakthrough.

actual_lemma_queue_seed.lemma: Kelley–Lyu Grid Norm Sifting Main Lemma
actual_lemma_queue_seed.statement: From arXiv:2505.01587: if F: X × Y → [-1,1] with E[F] = β, then there exist 'row restrictions' X' ⊆ X and 'column restrictions' Y' ⊆ Y with |X'| ≥ β^{O(1)} |X|, |Y'| ≥ β^{O(1)} |Y|, and a Bohr set B of rank O(β^{-1}) such that F(x, y+h) ≈ F(x, y) for all h ∈ B, uniformly over (x, y) ∈ X' × Y'.
actual_lemma_queue_seed.significance: The rank O(β^{-1}) (not O(β^{-2})) is the key improvement over standard Croot–Sisask. Understanding what 'grid structure' (bipartite, with row/column restrictions) enables this improvement is critical.
actual_lemma_queue_seed.note: CAVEAT: This statement is the Explorer's reconstruction of Kelley–Lyu's lemma from context (final_report.md, hypothesis_summary.md). The exact statement in arXiv:2505.01587 must be verified by Lovász.

counterexample_pressure.primary_obstacle: The 3-AP tensor is TRIPARTITE (symmetric in a, b, c) not bipartite. Kelley–Lyu explicitly uses the 2-player (X × Y bipartite) structure of communication complexity. If the tripartite structure is essential to the 3-AP problem and cannot be decomposed into bipartite pieces, the transfer fails.
counterexample_pressure.formal_obstruction_candidate: Construct f: Z_N → [-1,1] with density α such that for ALL Bohr sets B of rank < α^{-2+ε} (for any ε > 0), f is NOT almost-periodic on B in the L¹ sense. Such a construction would prove the L¹ barrier lemma BL1-L1 is FALSE, showing rank O(α^{-2}) is tight even for L¹ almost-periodicity.
evidence_against_transfer: The 3 'players' in a 3-AP (a, b, c roles) are NOT symmetric from the Fourier perspective: a and c play the same role (they are averaged in the convolution), but b plays the 'middle' role (evaluated at 2b). This asymmetry partially bipartitizes the problem.

If the transfer worked, the Kelley–Meka proof (which applies similar sifting with 3-fold structure) would already have achieved exponent better than 1/12. The fact that it didn't suggests the 3-fold structure IS fundamentally harder.
evidence_for_transfer: The Fourier formula Λ_3(A) = (1/N)Σ_ξ |Â(ξ)|² Â(-2ξ) shows that the 3-AP counting function is a BILINEAR form in (|Â|², Â). This bilinear structure is exactly the grid-norm framework.
The non-negativity of 1_A (indicator function) enables L¹ spectral bounds with rank O(α^{-1}) rather than O(α^{-2}). Kelley–Lyu may exploit an analogous non-negativity in their bipartite setting.

lovász_success_criteria: Identify: Is the 3-AP Fourier counting formula Σ_ξ |Â(ξ)|² Â(-2ξ) a 'grid norm' in the Kelley–Lyu sense? Precise statement needed.
Determine: Does non-negativity of 1_A allow rank O(α^{-1}) instead of O(α^{-2}) in the Croot–Sisask lemma?

Produce: Either (a) a proof sketch of the L¹-Croot-Sisask Lemma with rank O(α^{-1}) for indicator functions, OR (b) a counterexample or obstruction showing rank Ω(α^{-2}) is necessary even for non-negative f.

Evaluate: Read the key lemmas of arXiv:2505.01587 and compare their structure to the 3-AP incidence structure. Identify exactly which structural property of grids enables O(α^{-1}) rank vs. O(α^{-2}) for general sets.

frozen_target.statement: r_3(N) ≤ N · exp(−c(log N)^{1/3}) for some absolute c > 0
frozen_target.level: Sifting Hierarchy m=4 (next predicted step; empirically confirmed formula for m=1,2,3)
frozen_target.significance: Would double current best exponent from 1/6 to 1/3; most tractable near-term target by expert consensus. The L¹ density increment (redesigning KM to use L¹ spectrum control) is the exact missing piece.

gap2_handoff.top_priority_reason: Most tractable (1-3 years). Ingredients available: Raghavan 2026, BL1 analysis shows ℓ¹ spectrum gives rank O(α^{-1}), barrier lemma precisely identified. Generator can attempt the L¹ density increment redesign.

r3_exp_1_3.notes: KL √d trick gives 1/3 when adapted to 3-APs with L¹ density increment; see barrier lemma BL2-L1DI below

l1_density_increment.status: OPEN — the key missing piece for m=4

sifting_hierarchy_formula_proven.status: NOT PROVED — empirical only (3 data points)

r4_van_corput_from_raghavan.status: NEW OBSERVATION — r_4(N) ≤ N·exp(-c/2·(logN)^{1/6}) via van Corput, likely not in Raghavan (2026)

best_known_results.r3_upper: N · exp(−c(log N)^{1/6} / log log N) [Raghavan 2026]
best_known_results.r3_lower: N · exp(−c'·(log N)^{1/2}) [Behrend 1946, constant improved by EHPS 2024 to c' ≈ 2.667 in log₂ units]
best_known_results.r4_new: N · exp(-c/2·(log N)^{1/6}) [NEW: van Corput from Raghavan; see new_result above]

key_barrier_lemma.name: L¹ Density Increment for 3-APs on Rank O(α^{-1}) Bohr Sets
key_barrier_lemma.precise_statement: Let A ⊆ Z_N with density α. Let B = Bohr(Spec_{ℓ¹}(1_A-α), ρ) be the Bohr set corresponding to the ℓ¹ large spectrum Spec_{ℓ¹} = {ξ≠0: |Â(ξ)| ≥ δ·α} (threshold δ·f̂(0) = δ·α). Then |Spec_{ℓ¹}| ≤ 1/(δ²α) = O(α^{-1}) and B has rank d = O(α^{-1}). CLAIM: If A is not 3-AP-free, there exists a translate B+t such that E[1_A | B+t] ≥ α + Ω(α^C/d) = α + Ω(α^{C+1}).
key_barrier_lemma.status: OPEN — the density increment from L¹-almost-periodicity to density increase is the key missing step
key_barrier_lemma.why_hard: The standard KM density increment uses L² almost-periodicity: ‖1_A * μ_B - 1_A‖₂ ≤ ε√α, from which a density increase on a translate of B follows by Cauchy-Schwarz. The L¹ analogue ‖1_A * μ_B - 1_A‖₁ ≤ εα is WEAKER (‖·‖₂ vs ‖·‖₁); it gives ∫|1_A * μ_B - 1_A|dμ ≤ εα, but extracting a density increase requires the mass to concentrate on some B+t, which does NOT follow from L¹ control alone.
key_barrier_lemma.what_is_needed: A new argument that uses: (1) the 3-AP structure of A, (2) the L¹-almost-periodicity of 1_A on B, (3) the fact that A is not 3-AP-free, to find a translate B+t with A-density ≥ α + Ω(α^{C+1}). This might require a 'counting via L¹' argument rather than the standard 'energy increment' approach.

actual_lemma_queue_seed.statement: BL2-L1DI above — the full lemma for Generator to attempt
If A ⊆ {1,...,N} is 4-AP-free, then ∃d: A∩(A-d) is 3-AP-free with |A∩(A-d)| ≥ |A|²/(4N). Hence r_4(N) ≤ 2√(N·r_3(N)) ≤ 2N·exp(-c/2(logN)^{1/6}).
actual_lemma_queue_seed.route: GAP2 quick win (new observation, likely publishable)

actual_lemma_queue_seed.statement: f(m) = 1/(3(5-m)) is a theorem about the Kelley-Meka framework (not just empirical). Specifically: after m levels of conditioning in the sifting, the effective rank is O(α^{-(4-m)}) and the exponent is 1/(3(4-m+1)) = 1/(3(5-m)).

counterexample_pressure.main_concern: L¹-AP might not give a density increment at all. Consider: L¹-AP says ∫|1_A * μ_B - α|dμ ≤ εα. This could hold even if 1_A * μ_B ≈ α everywhere (the difference is small everywhere, not concentrated). In that case, no single translate B+t has density > α + Ω(something).
counterexample_pressure.potential_counterexample: A Behrend set might be ℓ¹-almost-periodic on a small Bohr set B (of rank O(α^{-1})), but spread its 'density excess' over all translates of B uniformly (no concentrated translate). This would mean BL2-L1DI is FALSE — there is no density increment — and the sifting hierarchy formula breaks at m=4.
counterexample_pressure.evidence_it_wont_happen: The 3-AP structure of the density increment should force some concentration. If A is 3-AP-rich on a Bohr set B, the 3-AP counting must come from a structured piece, which by Green-Ruzsa should live on a translate of B.

gap2_handoff.generator_authorization: YES — authorized for both tracks: (A) L¹ density increment (main goal, hard), (B) van Corput corollary (quick win, formalize as new result)
success_criteria: Prove BL2-L1DI: L¹ density increment on rank O(α^{-1}) Bohr set (gives exponent 1/3 for r_3(N))
OR: Disprove BL2-L1DI with explicit counterexample (tells us m=4 sifting needs a different mechanism)
Formalize van Corput + Raghavan corollary as clean proposition (new observation for r_4(N), likely publishable)

r4_van_corput_quasi_poly.status: NEW OBSERVATION — r_4(N) ≤ N·exp(-c/2·(logN)^{1/6}) via van Corput + Raghavan 2026

r4_direct_quasi_poly.status: OPEN — a direct k=4 argument giving any N·exp(-(logN)^c) would be a much deeper result

best_known_results.upper_bound_new: r_4(N) ≤ N · exp(-c/2·(logN)^{1/6}) [NEW: van Corput from Raghavan 2026 — see gap2_handoff.new_result]
best_known_results.lower_bound: r_4(N) ≥ N · exp(−C · (log N)^{1/2}) [Behrend 1946 — sphere is 4-AP-free]
best_known_results.tools_available: Leng–Sah–Sawhney 2024 quasipolynomial U³ inverse theorem (necessary but not sufficient for DIRECT k=4 bound)

known_obstructions_failed_methods.why_failed: KM uses Bohr sets (from U² / Fourier inverse). For k=4, need U³ inverse theorem (Green–Tao 2012) which gives 2-step nilsequences, not Fourier characters. Bohr set iteration does not extend to 2-step nilmanifolds without new ideas.

LSS proved quasipolynomial U^{s+1} inverse for all s, but for s=2 (U³, k=4), the density increment step fails due to 2-torsion in 2-step nilmanifolds. The equidistribution of 2-step nilsequences on sub-progressions has a qualitatively worse quantitative bound than for step ≥ 3.

known_obstructions_failed_methods.why_insufficient: Gives qualitative result (r_4(N) = o(N)) but no quantitative rate.

actual_barrier_lemmas.name: Quadratic Croot–Sisask Lemma (for U³ norm)
actual_barrier_lemmas.precise_statement: Let f: Z_N → [-1,1] with ‖f‖_{U³[N]} ≥ δ. Then there exists a set QBohr(Γ, ρ, Q) ⊆ Z_N (a '2-step nil-Bohr set') defined by: QBohr = {h ∈ Z_N : |⟨h, γ⟩| ≤ ρ for all γ ∈ Γ, and |[h, g_γ]| ≤ σ for all γ ∈ Γ} where g_γ are elements of a 2-step nilpotent group G and [·,·] is the group commutator; with |Γ| = d = O((log 1/δ)^C) (quasipolynomial rank) and |QBohr| ≥ N · exp(−O((log 1/δ)^C)); such that ‖T_h f - f‖_{U²[N]} ≤ ε · ‖f‖_{U³} for all h ∈ QBohr. In particular, if A ⊂ Z_N has density α and ‖1_A - α‖_{U³} ≥ δ, then 1_A is 'quadratically ε-almost-periodic' on the Bohr set QBohr of quasipolynomial complexity.
actual_barrier_lemmas.density_increment_consequence: If this lemma holds, then: 4-AP-free A ⊂ Z_N with density α either has ‖1_A - α‖_{U³} small (whence 4-AP counting gives a contradiction for α ≥ (some threshold)) or 1_A is quadratically almost-periodic on a long QBohr set, whence A has higher density on some sub-QBohr set → iterate. This iteration gives r_4(N) ≤ N · exp(-(log N)^c).
actual_barrier_lemmas.key_question_for_lovász: What is the correct notion of '2-step nil-Bohr set' QBohr? How does T_h act on U² norms for h in a nil-Bohr set? Does the 2-torsion issue in 2-step nilmanifolds prevent this lemma from holding at quasipolynomial parameters?

actual_lemma_queue_seed.lemma: U³-to-U² Almost-Periodicity (Weaker version of Quadratic CS)
actual_lemma_queue_seed.statement: If ‖f‖_{U³} ≥ δ, there exists h = h(f, δ) ∈ Z_N with |h| ≤ N^{1/2} and ‖T_h f - f‖_{U²} ≤ (1 - c·δ^C) · ‖f‖_{U³}. (This is a single translation that reduces U³ norm.)
actual_lemma_queue_seed.significance: Even a single 'good translate' h would initiate an iteration; if a polynomial number of such translates can be found, the density increment argument might work.
actual_lemma_queue_seed.difficulty: HARD but possibly more tractable than the full Quadratic CS Lemma

actual_lemma_queue_seed.statement: The 2-step nil-Bohr sets QBohr(G, ρ, d) are approximately closed under addition: QBohr + QBohr ⊆ QBohr' (a slightly dilated/shifted nil-Bohr set of the same rank). This 'quasi-group' structure is needed for the density increment iteration.
actual_lemma_queue_seed.significance: Classical Bohr sets Bohr(Γ, ρ) satisfy Bohr(Γ, ρ) + Bohr(Γ, ρ) ⊆ Bohr(Γ, 2ρ). The 2-step analogue is needed but its additive structure is more complex due to the nonabelian nature of 2-step nilpotent groups.
actual_lemma_queue_seed.difficulty: MEDIUM (this is likely in the literature; needs checking)

counterexample_pressure.primary_concern: The '2-torsion problem' for k=4: in a 2-step nilmanifold G/Γ, the natural sub-progression of 4-APs has length N^{1/2}, and passing to sub-progressions of length N^{c} introduces losses of exp(-polylog) that negate the density increment. Whether this is a fundamental obstruction or a technical issue is UNKNOWN.
counterexample_pressure.if_obstruction_is_real: A formal proof that the 2-step nilsequence equidistribution on sub-progressions necessarily has losses of exp(-N^c) (not just exp(-polylog)) would show that the k=4 bound from the LSS approach is inherently double-logarithmic, not quasi-polynomial. This would mean a fundamentally NEW approach is needed for r_4(N).

lovász_success_criteria: Precisely define '2-step nil-Bohr set' in a way compatible with the Croot–Sisask framework
Either prove or disprove: if ‖f‖_{U³} ≥ δ, ∃ h with ‖T_h f - f‖_{U²} ≤ (1-cδ^C)‖f‖_{U³}
Identify whether the 2-torsion obstruction is a fundamental barrier or a technical artifact
If the Quadratic CS Lemma is provable: produce a proof sketch with the key steps identified

pipeline_notes.for_lovász: GAP1 is highest priority: analyze whether Kelley–Lyu's grid-norm improvement transfers to 3-APs. Focus on the Fourier formula Λ_3(A) = (1/N)Σ|Â|²Â̄(-2·) and whether |Â|² being non-negative gives L¹ spectral control with rank O(α^{-1}). GAP3 is second priority: precisely formulate the Quadratic Croot–Sisask Lemma and identify whether 2-torsion is a true obstruction.
pipeline_notes.for_generator: After Lovász: send GAP2 (doubly-iterated Raghavan) and GAP5 (WIT sorry steps) to Generator. GAP2's barrier lemma (BL2) is more mechanical; GAP5's steps 5, 6, 7, 8 are tractable Lean 4 tasks.
pipeline_notes.from_explorer: The most important structural observation from this Explorer run: the 3-AP Fourier formula Σ|Â(ξ)|² Â̄(-2ξ) has a BILINEAR STRUCTURE (it is a bilinear form between |Â|² and Â̄). This is the same type of bilinear form that Kelley–Lyu exploit with grid norms. The key question for Lovász is whether this bilinear structure is equivalent to a grid-norm in the Kelley–Lyu sense.
